summary: Luu recounts an interview where he disagreed with an interviewer who believed PCA (Principal Component Analysis) was a universal solution for all classification problems. Using empirical examples comparing LSA (a linear dimensionality reduction technique) to deep learning autoencoders for text classification, he demonstrates that linear methods have fundamental limitations when dealing with nuanced, complex patterns. The post critiques the broader trend of data scientists mechanically applying a small set of linear techniques to every problem, regardless of whether those methods are appropriate for the problem's inherent structure.
key_points: PCA and similar linear dimensionality reduction methods cannot effectively capture complex, non-linear patterns in data
Deep learning methods significantly outperform linear techniques like LSA for nuanced classification tasks
Many self-described data scientists overuse a small toolkit of linear methods inappropriately
Real-world system performance contains non-linear behavior that cannot be captured by simple regression models
Engineers should apply the same skepticism to generic data science approaches as they do to management consultants
money_quotes: When all you have are linear hammers, non-linear screws look a lot like nails.
But data science is new and hot enough that people get a pass when they call themselves data scientists instead of technology consultants.
I keep seeing people try to apply the same four linear methods to every problem in sight.
Real system performance is full of non-linear cliffs, the sort of thing where you can't just apply a queuing theory model, let alone a linear regression model.
key_insight: The popularity of data science has led many practitioners to mechanically apply simple linear techniques to complex problems that fundamentally require non-linear approaches, producing poor results with misplaced confidence.
